ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pakoda or Indian vegetable fritters; 'fritter (food product)' is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar deep-fried battered products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated pakoda term; A0CKP (fritters and similar deep-fried battered products) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pakoda or Indian vegetable fritters.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (onion, potato, spinach, cauliflower, green chilli)
spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mix vegetable pakoda consists of approximately 4 to 5 pieces weighing around 120 grams, often served with mint chutney or tamarind sauce as a snack or starter.

nutrient_source.title: Pakora (vegetable fritter) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central reference for chickpea flour and deep-fried vegetable fritter composition
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for pakora/bhajia (vegetable fritter in chickpea batter, deep-fried). Values adapted for a mixed-vegetable variant using standard besan-batter composition. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for chickpea-flour-battered deep-fried vegetable fritters. Per-100g values represent the cooked, deep-fried product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chickpea-flour-based deep-fried snacks; analogous estimate based on besan (chickpea flour) fritter GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on legume-flour-battered fried foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mix vegetable pakoda in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated based on chickpea-flour (besan) battered deep-fried snacks, which have a GI in the range of 48–55 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Chickpea flour has a low GI due to its high protein and fibre content, which slows starch digestion. The addition of mixed vegetables does not materially raise the GI. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 29.8 × 1.20) / 100 = 18.5 g for a 120 g serving. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.guidance: Mix vegetable pakoda contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as commercial tamarind chutney to keep total sodium within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load and fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the chickpea-flour batter confers a low glycemic index (estimated GI 52), the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at approximately 18.5 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 2–3 pieces and pair with a low-GI accompaniment such as plain yoghurt rather than sweet chutneys to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat and energy density from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying in vegetable oil contributes approximately 14.5 g fat per 100 g, raising energy density; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict intake to occasional small servings and prefer air-fried or oven-baked variants to reduce total and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mix Vegetable Pakoda (also spelled pakora) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made by coating an assortment of vegetables — commonly onion, potato, spinach, cauliflower, and green chilli — in a spiced chickpea-flour (besan) batter and frying until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy and lightly spiced with cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli powder, while the interior remains soft and moist from the steamed vegetables. Pakoda is a staple street food and home snack across the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (approximately 268 kcal per 100 g) primarily from carbohydrate and fat, with a meaningful protein contribution from the chickpea-flour batter. The chickpea flour base gives it a relatively low glycemic index (estimated GI 52) compared to wheat-flour-based snacks, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate due to portion size. The sodium content is notable at around 310 mg per 100 g, and the fat content reflects the deep-frying method. Clinically, it is a moderate-concern food for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, and portion control is advised for individuals managing these conditions. It is commonly served with mint-coriander chutney or tamarind sauce as a starter, tea-time snack, or side dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable pakora?
What is the GI of pakoda?
What vegetables are in mix veg pakoda?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for mix vegetable pakoda was found in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from the closest matching ICMR-NIN pakora/bhajia entry (vegetable fritter in chickpea batter, deep-fried) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central chickpea-flour fritter composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for chickpea-flour-based fried snacks. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) pakora/bhajia entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central. No exact 'mix vegetable pakoda' entry exists in any approved primary source; values represent the best defensible estimate for a standard besan-battered mixed-vegetable deep-fried pakoda. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as pakoda is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived ingredients; this is naturally zero for a vegan food product. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based deep-fried snack with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per food composition logic. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the product is fried in standard vegetable oil with no hydrogenated fat; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA reference data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category and is not expected to be present in a plant-based fried snack; value is -1 as not reported in the source database. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category; value is -1 as not reported in the source database. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea-flour-battered deep-fried snack data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mix vegetable pakoda was found. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 5. The field survey data source price of SGD 17.00 from Dugout - Circular Road is noted but not used as a nutritional evidence source. 6. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact pakoda term exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2. 7. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
